## evo/policy/per-packet-load-balance.conf

```
/*
 * Topic:   Per-packet load-balance policy for PFE forwarding-table export
 * Variant: Evolved-OS (EVO)
 * Seen on:
 *   Junos: esi-leaf1_qfx5120-48y esi-leaf2_qfx5120-48y server-leaf1_qfx5120-48y borderleaf1_qfx5120-32c borderleaf2_qfx5120-32c
 *   EVO:   spine1_qfx5220-32cd spine2_qfx5220-32cd borderleaf1_qfx5130-32cd borderleaf2_qfx5130-32cd
 *
 * Highlights:
 *  - Body is byte-identical to the Junos sibling.
 *  - Generated by Juniper Apstra — identical on all devices regardless of role or OS.
 *  - "per-packet" means per-flow hash-based ECMP (not true per-packet round-robin).
 *
 * Pair with:
 *  - evo/transport/forwarding-table-ecmp.conf  (references this policy)
 */
policy-options {
    policy-statement PFE-LB {
        then {
            load-balance per-packet;
        }
    }
}
```

## evo/policy/pod-network-export.conf

```
/*
 * Topic:   AllPodNetworks direct-route export policy with per-VRF community tagging
 * Variant: Evolved-OS (EVO)
 * Seen on:
 *   Junos: esi-leaf1_qfx5120-48y esi-leaf2_qfx5120-48y server-leaf1_qfx5120-48y borderleaf1_qfx5120-32c borderleaf2_qfx5120-32c
 *   EVO:   spine1_qfx5220-32cd spine2_qfx5220-32cd borderleaf1_qfx5130-32cd borderleaf2_qfx5130-32cd
 *
 * Highlights:
 *  - Body is byte-identical to the Junos sibling.
 *  - Generated by Juniper Apstra — per-VRF variants tag direct routes for
 *    EVPN ip-prefix-routes export filtering.
 *
 * Pair with:
 *  - evo/policy/bgp-aos-export-policy.conf  (chains from-policy AllPodNetworks)
 *  - evo/policy/community-definitions.conf  (community values)
 *
 * Variables (example values from borderleaf1_qfx5130-32cd):
 *   $VRF_NAME          e.g. blue
 *   $VRF_COMMUNITY_V4  e.g. BLUE_COMMUNITY_V4
 *   $VRF_COMMUNITY_V6  e.g. BLUE_COMMUNITY_V6
 */
policy-options {
    policy-statement AllPodNetworks {
        term AllPodNetworks-10 {
            from {
                family inet;
                protocol direct;
            }
            then {
                community add DEFAULT_DIRECT_V4;
                accept;
            }
        }
        term AllPodNetworks-20 {
            from {
                family inet6;
                protocol direct;
            }
            then {
                community add DEFAULT_DIRECT_V6;
                accept;
            }
        }
        term AllPodNetworks-100 {
            then reject;
        }
    }
    policy-statement AllPodNetworks-$VRF_NAME {
        term AllPodNetworks-$VRF_NAME-10 {
            from {
                family inet;
                protocol direct;
            }
            then {
                community add $VRF_COMMUNITY_V4;
                accept;
            }
        }
        term AllPodNetworks-$VRF_NAME-20 {
            from {
                family inet6;
                protocol direct;
            }
            then {
                community add $VRF_COMMUNITY_V6;
                accept;
            }
        }
        term AllPodNetworks-$VRF_NAME-100 {
            then reject;
        }
    }
}
```

## evo/policy/route-filter-lists.conf

```
/*
 * Topic:   Route-filter-list definitions for external peer import/export control
 * Variant: Evolved-OS (EVO)
 * Seen on:
 *   Junos: borderleaf1_qfx5120-32c borderleaf2_qfx5120-32c
 *   EVO:   borderleaf1_qfx5130-32cd borderleaf2_qfx5130-32cd
 *
 * Highlights:
 *  - Body is byte-identical to the Junos sibling.
 *  - Generated by Juniper Apstra — route-filter-list syntax is OS-independent.
 *
 * Pair with:
 *  - evo/policy/external-route-import.conf  (references RoutesFromExt lists)
 *  - evo/policy/external-route-export.conf  (references RoutesToExt lists)
 *
 * Variables (example values from borderleaf1_qfx5130-32cd):
 *   $VRF_NAME       e.g. blue
 *   $EXT_PEER_NAME  e.g. external_router
 *   $PREFIX/$LEN    site-specific prefix entries
 */
policy-options {
    /* Import filter — accept only default route from external peer */
    route-filter-list RoutesFromExt-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME {
        0.0.0.0/0 exact;
    }
    route-filter-list RoutesFromExtV6-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME {
        0::0/0 exact;
    }

    /* Export filter — advertise specific internal prefixes to external peer */
    route-filter-list RoutesToExt-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME {
        0.0.0.0/0 prefix-length-range /1-/32;
        $PREFIX/$LEN upto /32;
        /* ... additional site-specific entries ... */
    }
    route-filter-list RoutesToExtV6-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME {
        ::/0 prefix-length-range /1-/127;
        $PREFIX6/$LEN6 upto /128;
        /* ... additional site-specific entries ... */
    }
}
```

## evo/services/vrf-evpn-ip-prefix.conf

```
/*
 * Topic:   VRF instance with EVPN ip-prefix-routes, optional eBGP external peering, and DHCP relay
 * Variant: Evolved-OS (EVO)
 * Seen on:
 *   Junos: esi-leaf1_qfx5120-48y esi-leaf2_qfx5120-48y server-leaf1_qfx5120-48y borderleaf1_qfx5120-32c borderleaf2_qfx5120-32c
 *   EVO:   borderleaf1_qfx5130-32cd borderleaf2_qfx5130-32cd
 *
 * Highlights:
 *  - Body is byte-identical to the Junos sibling.
 *  - Generated by Juniper Apstra — VRF with EVPN Type-5 ip-prefix-routes.
 *
 * Pair with:
 *  - evo/policy/bgp-aos-export-policy.conf     (BGP-AOS-Policy-$VRF_NAME)
 *  - evo/policy/external-route-import.conf      (import on l3rtr neighbors)
 *  - evo/policy/external-route-export.conf      (export on l3rtr neighbors)
 *  - evo/interfaces/irb-gateway.conf            (IRB interfaces bound here)
 *  - evo/interfaces/external-vlan-tagged.conf      (external-vlan-tagged)
 *  - evo/interfaces/loopback-multi-unit.conf       (loopback-multi-unit)
 *  - evo/oam/ipv6-router-advertisement.conf        (ipv6-router-advertisement)
 *
 *
 * JVD service mapping:
 *   3 instances total (high 3 / med 0 / low 0)
 *   On devices: esi-leaf1_qfx5120-48y (3), esi-leaf2_qfx5120-48y (3), server-leaf1_qfx5120-48y (3)
 *   Example: blue (RD 192.168.255.4:3, RT target:20002:1)
 *     esi-leaf1_qfx5120-48y
 *     esi-leaf2_qfx5120-48y
 *     server-leaf1_qfx5120-48y
 *
 * Variables (example values from borderleaf1_qfx5130-32cd VRF "blue"):
 *   $VRF_NAME          e.g. blue
 *   $VNI               e.g. 20002
 *   $RD                e.g. 192.168.255.2:3
 *   $VRF_TARGET        e.g. target:20002:1
 *   $LO_UNIT           e.g. 3
 *   $DHCP_SERVER       e.g. 10.10.0.200
 *   $EXT_NEIGHBOR_V4   e.g. 10.200.0.5
 *   $EXT_LOCAL_V4      e.g. 10.200.0.4
 *   $EXT_NEIGHBOR_V6   e.g. 2001:db8:dc1:10:200::5
 *   $EXT_LOCAL_V6      e.g. 2001:db8:dc1:10:200::4
 *   $EXT_PEER_AS       e.g. 65000
 *   $EXT_PEER_NAME     e.g. external_router
 *   $WAN_INTERFACE     e.g. et-0/0/8:0.399
 */
routing-instances {
    $VRF_NAME {
        instance-type vrf;
        routing-options {
            rib $VRF_NAME.inet6.0 {
                multipath;
            }
            graceful-restart;
            multipath;
            auto-export;
        }
        protocols {
            bgp {
                group l3rtr {
                    type external;
                    multihop {
                        ttl 1;
                    }
                    family inet {
                        unicast {
                            loops 2;
                        }
                    }
                    family inet6 {
                        unicast {
                            loops 2;
                        }
                    }
                    multipath {
                        multiple-as;
                    }
                    neighbor $EXT_NEIGHBOR_V6 {
                        description facing_$EXT_PEER_NAME;
                        multihop {
                            ttl 2;
                        }
                        local-address $EXT_LOCAL_V6;
                        import ( RoutesFromExt-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME );
                        family inet6 {
                            unicast;
                        }
                        export ( RoutesToExt-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME );
                        peer-as $EXT_PEER_AS;
                    }
                    neighbor $EXT_NEIGHBOR_V4 {
                        description facing_$EXT_PEER_NAME;
                        multihop {
                            ttl 2;
                        }
                        local-address $EXT_LOCAL_V4;
                        import ( RoutesFromExt-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME );
                        family inet {
                            unicast;
                        }
                        export ( RoutesToExt-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME );
                        peer-as $EXT_PEER_AS;
                    }
                }
                graceful-restart {
                    dont-help-shared-fate-bfd-down;
                }
            }
            evpn {
                ip-prefix-routes {
                    advertise direct-nexthop;
                    encapsulation vxlan;
                    vni $VNI;
                    export BGP-AOS-Policy-$VRF_NAME;
                }
            }
        }
        forwarding-options {
            dhcp-relay {
                forward-only;
                server-group {
                    $VRF_NAME {
                        $DHCP_SERVER;
                    }
                }
                group $VRF_NAME {
                    active-server-group $VRF_NAME;
                    relay-option-82 {
                        server-id-override;
                    }
                }
            }
        }
        interface $WAN_INTERFACE;
        interface lo0.$LO_UNIT;
        route-distinguisher $RD;
        vrf-target $VRF_TARGET;
    }
}
```

## evo/transport/bgp-evpn-overlay.conf

```
/*
 * Topic:   eBGP EVPN overlay peer group with multihop and signaling
 * Variant: Evolved-OS (EVO)
 * Seen on:
 *   Junos: (none)
 *   EVO:   spine1_qfx5220-32cd spine2_qfx5220-32cd borderleaf1_qfx5130-32cd borderleaf2_qfx5130-32cd
 *
 * Highlights:
 *  - Generated by Juniper Apstra — group name "l3clos-l-evpn" (leaf) or
 *    "l3clos-s-evpn" (spine).
 *  - Overlay eBGP sessions use loopback addresses (multihop ttl 1) — decoupled
 *    from physical link state, survives individual link failures.
 *  - no-nexthop-change preserves the originating VTEP address through the spine.
 *  - family evpn signaling with loops 2 allows type-5 routes to re-enter the fabric
 *    for inter-VRF routing via border leaf.
 *  - BFD at 3000ms×3 (slower than underlay — overlay is multihop).
 *
 * Pair with:
 *  - evo/transport/bgp-underlay-fabric.conf      (underlay provides reachability)
 *  - evo/interfaces/loopback-multi-unit.conf     (lo0.0 used as local-address)
 *  - evo/policy/leaf-to-spine-evpn-filter.conf   (export policy applied here)
 *
 * Variables (example values from borderleaf1_qfx5130-32cd):
 *   $EVPN_GROUP        e.g. l3clos-l-evpn
 *   $OVERLAY_PEER      e.g. 192.168.255.0
 *   $OVERLAY_LOCAL     e.g. 192.168.255.2
 *   $PEER_DESCRIPTION  e.g. facing_spine1-evpn-overlay
 *   $PEER_AS           e.g. 64512
 *   $BFD_INTERVAL_MS   e.g. 3000
 *   $BFD_MULTIPLIER    e.g. 3
 */
protocols {
    bgp {
        group $EVPN_GROUP {
            type external;
            multihop {
                ttl 1;
                no-nexthop-change;
            }
            family evpn {
                signaling {
                    loops 2;
                }
            }
            multipath {
                multiple-as;
            }
            bfd-liveness-detection {
                minimum-interval $BFD_INTERVAL_MS;
                multiplier $BFD_MULTIPLIER;
            }
            neighbor $OVERLAY_PEER {
                description $PEER_DESCRIPTION;
                local-address $OVERLAY_LOCAL;
                family evpn {
                    signaling;
                }
                export ( LEAF_TO_SPINE_EVPN_OUT && EVPN_EXPORT );
                peer-as $PEER_AS;
            }
            vpn-apply-export;
        }
    }
}
```

## evo/transport/bgp-underlay-fabric.conf

```
/*
 * Topic:   eBGP underlay fabric peer group (leaf-to-spine or spine-to-leaf)
 * Variant: Evolved-OS (EVO)
 * Seen on:
 *   Junos: (none)
 *   EVO:   spine1_qfx5220-32cd spine2_qfx5220-32cd borderleaf1_qfx5130-32cd borderleaf2_qfx5130-32cd
 *
 * Highlights:
 *  - Generated by Juniper Apstra — group naming convention: "l3clos-s" for spine,
 *    "l3clos-l" for leaf. This template shows the leaf-to-spine variant.
 *  - eBGP with unique ASN per device (Apstra default: spine 64512/64513,
 *    leaf 64514–64518) — enables ECMP multipath with multiple-as.
 *  - BFD liveness at 1000ms×3 for sub-second failure detection on fabric links.
 *  - vpn-apply-export ensures VPN routes (EVPN) inherit the fabric export policy.
 *  - Spine variant adds SPINE_TO_LEAF_FABRIC_OUT export; leaf adds LEAF_TO_SPINE_FABRIC_OUT.
 *
 * Pair with:
 *  - evo/transport/bgp-evpn-overlay.conf         (overlay BGP uses same spine peers)
 *  - evo/policy/leaf-to-spine-fabric-filter.conf  (export policy applied here)
 *  - evo/policy/bgp-aos-export-policy.conf        (chained export policy)
 *  - evo/interfaces/fabric-uplink.conf            (physical links these sessions run over)
 *  - evo/transport/forwarding-table-ecmp.conf      (forwarding-table-ecmp)
 *
 * Variables (example values from borderleaf1_qfx5130-32cd):
 *   $BGP_GROUP         e.g. l3clos-l
 *   $PEER_IP           e.g. 10.0.1.0
 *   $LOCAL_IP          e.g. 10.0.1.1
 *   $PEER_DESCRIPTION  e.g. facing_spine1
 *   $PEER_AS           e.g. 64512
 *   $BFD_INTERVAL_MS   e.g. 1000
 *   $BFD_MULTIPLIER    e.g. 3
 */
protocols {
    bgp {
        group $BGP_GROUP {
            type external;
            multipath {
                multiple-as;
            }
            bfd-liveness-detection {
                minimum-interval $BFD_INTERVAL_MS;
                multiplier $BFD_MULTIPLIER;
            }
            neighbor $PEER_IP {
                description $PEER_DESCRIPTION;
                local-address $LOCAL_IP;
                family inet {
                    unicast;
                }
                export ( LEAF_TO_SPINE_FABRIC_OUT && BGP-AOS-Policy );
                peer-as $PEER_AS;
            }
            vpn-apply-export;
        }
        log-updown;
        graceful-restart {
            dont-help-shared-fate-bfd-down;
        }
        multipath;
    }
}
```

## evo/transport/forwarding-table-ecmp.conf

```
/*
 * Topic:   Forwarding-table ECMP with per-packet load-balance and chained-composite-next-hop
 * Variant: Evolved-OS (EVO)
 * Seen on:
 *   Junos: (none)
 *   EVO:   spine1_qfx5220-32cd spine2_qfx5220-32cd borderleaf1_qfx5130-32cd borderleaf2_qfx5130-32cd
 *
 * Highlights:
 *  - Generated by Juniper Apstra — standard ECMP tuning for EVPN/VXLAN fabrics.
 *  - export PFE-LB: applies the per-packet load-balance policy to all forwarding lookups.
 *  - ecmp-fast-reroute: pre-programs backup ECMP paths for sub-second convergence
 *    when a spine link fails.
 *  - chained-composite-next-hop ingress evpn: required on leaf/border-leaf for
 *    EVPN Type-5 symmetric routing — chains the VXLAN encap with the VRF lookup.
 *    Spine omits this block (no EVPN data-plane termination).
 *
 * Pair with:
 *  - evo/policy/per-packet-load-balance.conf  (PFE-LB policy referenced by export)
 *  - evo/transport/bgp-underlay-fabric.conf   (ECMP paths across spines)
 *
 * Variables (example values from borderleaf1_qfx5130-32cd):
 *   $ROUTER_ID     e.g. 192.168.255.2
 *   $LOCAL_AS      e.g. 64514
 */
routing-options {
    router-id $ROUTER_ID;
    autonomous-system $LOCAL_AS;
    graceful-restart;
    forwarding-table {
        export PFE-LB;
        ecmp-fast-reroute;
        chained-composite-next-hop {
            ingress {
                evpn;
            }
        }
    }
}
```

## junos/bootstrap/chassis-port-config.conf

```
/*
 * Topic:   Chassis aggregated-devices and FPC port-speed configuration for QFX5120 leaf
 * Seen on:
 *   Junos: esi-leaf1_qfx5120-48y esi-leaf2_qfx5120-48y server-leaf1_qfx5120-48y
 *   EVO:   (none)
 *
 * Highlights:
 *  - Generated by Juniper Apstra — device-count matches the number of LAG bundles
 *    provisioned by the Apstra intent model.
 *  - aggregated-devices ethernet device-count must be >= the highest aeN unit number
 *    in use (ae1, ae2 → device-count 2).
 *  - Uplink ports (et-0/0/48, et-0/0/49) are set to 100g for spine connectivity;
 *    access ports are 10g by default on QFX5120-48Y.
 *
 * Pair with:
 *  - junos/interfaces/lag-esi-access.conf  (LAGs that consume the aggregated-devices count)
 *  - junos/interfaces/fabric-uplink.conf   (uplink ports using the 100g speed)
 *  - junos/bootstrap/grpc-certificate.conf         (grpc-certificate)
 *
 * Variables (example values from esi-leaf1_qfx5120-48y):
 *   $LAG_DEVICE_COUNT   e.g. 2
 *   $UPLINK_PORT_1      e.g. 48
 *   $UPLINK_PORT_2      e.g. 49
 *   $UPLINK_SPEED       e.g. 100g
 */
chassis {
    aggregated-devices {
        ethernet {
            device-count $LAG_DEVICE_COUNT;
        }
    }
    fpc 0 {
        pic 0 {
            port $UPLINK_PORT_1 {
                speed $UPLINK_SPEED;
            }
            port $UPLINK_PORT_2 {
                speed $UPLINK_SPEED;
            }
        }
    }
}
```

## junos/bootstrap/grpc-certificate.conf

```
/*
 * Topic:   gRPC TLS certificate for Juniper Apstra controller authentication
 * Seen on:
 *   Junos: esi-leaf1_qfx5120-48y esi-leaf2_qfx5120-48y server-leaf1_qfx5120-48y borderleaf1_qfx5120-32c borderleaf2_qfx5120-32c
 *   EVO:   (none)
 *
 * Highlights:
 *  - Body is byte-identical to the EVO sibling.
 *  - Generated by Juniper Apstra — the certificate name "aos_grpc" is the Apstra
 *    convention (AOS = Apstra Operating System, the former product name).
 *  - SECRET-DATA placeholder replaces the actual PEM-encoded cert+key at commit time.
 *
 * Pair with:
 *  - junos/bootstrap/chassis-port-config.conf  (same bootstrap scope)
 *
 * Variables (example values from esi-leaf1_qfx5120-48y):
 *   $CERT_NAME       e.g. aos_grpc
 *   $SECRET_DATA     e.g. (PEM certificate — replaced by "secret" ## SECRET-DATA)
 */
security {
    certificates {
        local {
            $CERT_NAME {
                "$SECRET_DATA"; ## SECRET-DATA
            }
        }
    }
}
```

## junos/interfaces/external-vlan-tagged.conf

```
/*
 * Topic:   Flexible-VLAN-tagged external interface for inter-VRF/WAN exit
 * Seen on:
 *   Junos: borderleaf1_qfx5120-32c borderleaf2_qfx5120-32c
 *   EVO:   (none)
 *
 * Highlights:
 *  - Generated by Juniper Apstra — Junos QFX5120-32C variant uses xe-0/0/0 as the
 *    external-facing port (no breakout needed, native 10G).
 *  - flexible-vlan-tagging with per-VRF VLAN units (same logical pattern as EVO sibling).
 *  - Includes EVPN-VXLAN forwarding-options (shared-tunnels, vxlan-routing) that are
 *    configured at the top level on Junos — not present on EVO border leafs which
 *    don't terminate VXLAN tunnels for fabric VLANs.
 *
 * Pair with:
 *  - junos/interfaces/irb-gateway.conf          (IRB pattern for VRF L3 exit)
 *  - junos/services/vrf-evpn-ip-prefix.conf     (VRF references these interface units)
 *  - junos/oam/ipv6-router-advertisement.conf      (ipv6-router-advertisement)
 *
 * Variables (example values from borderleaf1_qfx5120-32c):
 *   $EXT_INTF          e.g. xe-0/0/0
 *   $NATIVE_VLAN       e.g. 1
 */
interfaces {
    $EXT_INTF {
        flexible-vlan-tagging;
        native-vlan-id $NATIVE_VLAN;
    }
}
```

## junos/interfaces/fabric-uplink.conf

```
/*
 * Topic:   Point-to-point fabric uplink to spine with /31 IPv4 addressing
 * Seen on:
 *   Junos: esi-leaf1_qfx5120-48y esi-leaf2_qfx5120-48y server-leaf1_qfx5120-48y borderleaf1_qfx5120-32c borderleaf2_qfx5120-32c
 *   EVO:   (none)
 *
 * Highlights:
 *  - Body is structurally identical to the EVO sibling (no "speed" knob on Junos
 *    QFX5120 — speed is set in chassis fpc/pic/port stanza instead).
 *  - Generated by Juniper Apstra — description follows "facing_<peer>:<port>" convention.
 *  - MTU 9216 at physical layer, 9170 at IP layer (46 bytes for L2 overhead).
 *  - QFX5120-48Y uses et-0/0/48, et-0/0/49 for 100G spine uplinks.
 *    QFX5120-32C uses et-0/0/48 for spine uplinks.
 *
 * Pair with:
 *  - junos/transport/bgp-underlay-fabric.conf  (BGP sessions run over these links)
 *  - junos/bootstrap/chassis-port-config.conf  (port speed assignment)
 *
 * Variables (example values from esi-leaf1_qfx5120-48y):
 *   $FABRIC_INTF       e.g. et-0/0/48
 *   $PEER_DESCRIPTION  e.g. facing_spine1:et-0/0/1
 *   $LINK_MTU          e.g. 9216
 *   $IP_MTU            e.g. 9170
 *   $LOCAL_IP_SLASH31  e.g. 10.0.1.5/31
 */
interfaces {
    $FABRIC_INTF {
        description $PEER_DESCRIPTION;
        mtu $LINK_MTU;
        unit 0 {
            family inet {
                mtu $IP_MTU;
                address $LOCAL_IP_SLASH31;
            }
        }
    }
}
```

## junos/interfaces/irb-gateway.conf

```
/*
 * Topic:   IRB gateway interface for EVPN/VXLAN symmetric routing (ERB model)
 * Seen on:
 *   Junos: esi-leaf1_qfx5120-48y esi-leaf2_qfx5120-48y server-leaf1_qfx5120-48y borderleaf1_qfx5120-32c borderleaf2_qfx5120-32c
 *   EVO:   (none)
 *
 * Highlights:
 *  - Generated by Juniper Apstra — one IRB unit per VLAN/VNI in the Edge-Routed
 *    Bridging (ERB) overlay; this snip shows the repeating per-VLAN pattern.
 *  - Dual-stack (inet + inet6) with 9000-byte MTU for jumbo frame support.
 *  - Static MAC (00:1c:73:00:00:01) shared across all leaf IRBs in the same VRF —
 *    this is the Apstra "anycast gateway" MAC enabling seamless VM mobility.
 *  - no-dhcp-flood on the first IRB unit (DHCP relay active on that subnet).
 *  - In practice hundreds of these units exist; template one and repeat per VLAN.
 *
 * Pair with:
 *  - junos/interfaces/vlan-vxlan-domain.conf  (vlans block maps VLAN to VNI + l3-interface)
 *  - junos/services/vrf-evpn-ip-prefix.conf   (VRF binds these IRB interfaces)
 *  - junos/oam/ipv6-router-advertisement.conf (IPv6 RA on each IRB unit)
 *  - junos/interfaces/external-vlan-tagged.conf    (external-vlan-tagged)
 *  - junos/services/mac-vrf-evpn-vxlan.conf        (mac-vrf-evpn-vxlan)
 *
 * Variables (example values from esi-leaf1_qfx5120-48y):
 *   $IRB_UNIT        e.g. 400
 *   $IRB_MTU         e.g. 9000
 *   $IRB_IPV4        e.g. 10.1.0.1/24
 *   $IRB_IPV6        e.g. 2001:db8:dc1:10:01:00::1/96
 *   $ANYCAST_MAC     e.g. 00:1c:73:00:00:01
 */
interfaces {
    irb {
        unit $IRB_UNIT {
            family inet {
                mtu $IRB_MTU;
                address $IRB_IPV4;
            }
            family inet6 {
                mtu $IRB_MTU;
                address $IRB_IPV6;
            }
            mac $ANYCAST_MAC;
        }
    }
}
```

## junos/interfaces/lag-esi-access.conf

```
/*
 * Topic:   LAG with EVPN ESI all-active multihoming for redundant server access
 * Seen on:
 *   Junos: esi-leaf1_qfx5120-48y esi-leaf2_qfx5120-48y
 *   EVO:   (none)
 *
 * Highlights:
 *  - Generated by Juniper Apstra — ESI value and LACP system-id follow Apstra's
 *    deterministic allocation scheme.
 *  - ESI Type 0 (manually configured) with all-active load-balancing across the
 *    ESI leaf pair — both leafs forward traffic for the same MAC.
 *  - LACP system-id is shared between the two ESI peers so the server sees a
 *    single logical LAG partner.
 *  - Trunk mode carries all VLAN members mapped to the tenant VNIs.
 *  - MTU 9216 for jumbo frame passthrough.
 *
 * Pair with:
 *  - junos/bootstrap/chassis-port-config.conf  (aggregated-devices device-count)
 *  - junos/interfaces/vlan-vxlan-domain.conf   (VLAN definitions referenced by trunk)
 *  - junos/interfaces/trunk-access-port.conf       (trunk-access-port)
 *  - junos/oam/rstp-bridge.conf                    (rstp-bridge)
 *  - junos/services/mac-vrf-evpn-vxlan.conf        (mac-vrf-evpn-vxlan)
 *
 * Variables (example values from esi-leaf1_qfx5120-48y):
 *   $AE_INTF            e.g. ae1
 *   $AE_DESCRIPTION     e.g. to.dc1-esi-001-sys003
 *   $AE_MTU             e.g. 9216
 *   $ESI_VALUE          e.g. 00:02:00:00:00:00:01:00:00:01
 *   $LACP_SYSTEM_ID     e.g. 02:00:00:00:00:01
 *   $VLAN_MEMBERS       e.g. [ vn400 vn401 ... ]
 */
interfaces {
    $AE_INTF {
        description $AE_DESCRIPTION;
        mtu $AE_MTU;
        esi {
            $ESI_VALUE;
            all-active;
        }
        aggregated-ether-options {
            lacp {
                active;
                system-id $LACP_SYSTEM_ID;
            }
        }
        unit 0 {
            family ethernet-switching {
                interface-mode trunk;
                vlan {
                    members $VLAN_MEMBERS;
                }
            }
        }
    }
}
```

## junos/interfaces/loopback-multi-unit.conf

```
/*
 * Topic:   Multi-unit loopback for router-id, EVPN overlay, and per-VRF identities
 * Seen on:
 *   Junos: esi-leaf1_qfx5120-48y esi-leaf2_qfx5120-48y server-leaf1_qfx5120-48y borderleaf1_qfx5120-32c borderleaf2_qfx5120-32c
 *   EVO:   (none)
 *
 * Highlights:
 *  - Body is byte-identical to the EVO sibling.
 *  - Generated by Juniper Apstra — unit allocation follows Apstra ID assignment.
 *  - Unit 0: router-id + underlay reachability (BGP overlay local-address source).
 *  - Units 2+: per-VRF loopback for route-distinguisher (one per tenant VRF).
 *  - Leaf nodes typically have 3–4 units (red, blue, green VRFs).
 *
 * Pair with:
 *  - junos/transport/bgp-evpn-overlay.conf  (overlay BGP uses lo0.0 address)
 *  - junos/services/vrf-evpn-ip-prefix.conf (VRF references lo0 unit for RD)
 *
 * Variables (example values from esi-leaf1_qfx5120-48y):
 *   $ROUTER_ID_V4     e.g. 192.168.255.4/32
 *   $ROUTER_ID_V6     e.g. fdf6:ed70:1fac:f2d1::1004/128
 *   $VRF_UNIT         e.g. 2
 *   $VRF_LO_V4        e.g. 192.168.11.4/32
 *   $VRF_LO_V6        e.g. fdf6:ed70:1fac:f2d1::2004/128
 */
interfaces {
    lo0 {
        unit 0 {
            family inet {
                address $ROUTER_ID_V4;
            }
            family inet6 {
                address $ROUTER_ID_V6;
            }
        }
        unit $VRF_UNIT {
            family inet {
                address $VRF_LO_V4;
            }
            family inet6 {
                address $VRF_LO_V6;
            }
        }
    }
}
```

## junos/interfaces/trunk-access-port.conf

```
/*
 * Topic:   L2 trunk access port carrying EVPN/VXLAN VLANs to servers
 * Seen on:
 *   Junos: esi-leaf1_qfx5120-48y esi-leaf2_qfx5120-48y server-leaf1_qfx5120-48y
 *   EVO:   (none)
 *
 * Highlights:
 *  - Generated by Juniper Apstra — VLAN members list is auto-populated from the
 *    Apstra connectivity template assigned to the server-facing port.
 *  - Single-homed access: no ESI, no LAG — used for standalone server connections.
 *  - MTU 9216 allows jumbo frames end-to-end within the VXLAN fabric.
 *  - In this JVD, the VLAN members list is large (~600 VLANs per VRF); template
 *    shows the pattern, not the full enumeration.
 *
 * Pair with:
 *  - junos/interfaces/lag-esi-access.conf   (multi-homed variant with ESI)
 *  - junos/interfaces/vlan-vxlan-domain.conf (VLAN definitions these ports reference)
 *
 * Variables (example values from esi-leaf1_qfx5120-48y):
 *   $ACCESS_INTF       e.g. xe-0/0/0
 *   $ACCESS_DESC       e.g. to.dc1-esi-001-sys001
 *   $ACCESS_MTU        e.g. 9216
 *   $VLAN_MEMBERS      e.g. [ vn400 vn401 vn402 ... ]
 */
interfaces {
    $ACCESS_INTF {
        description $ACCESS_DESC;
        mtu $ACCESS_MTU;
        unit 0 {
            family ethernet-switching {
                interface-mode trunk;
                vlan {
                    members $VLAN_MEMBERS;
                }
            }
        }
    }
}
```

## junos/interfaces/vlan-vxlan-domain.conf

```
/*
 * Topic:   VLAN-to-VXLAN domain mapping with VNI and optional L3 IRB interface
 * Seen on:
 *   Junos: esi-leaf1_qfx5120-48y esi-leaf2_qfx5120-48y server-leaf1_qfx5120-48y
 *   EVO:   (none)
 *
 * Highlights:
 *  - Generated by Juniper Apstra — VLAN names follow Apstra "vn<id>" convention
 *    with descriptions indicating VRF + subnet purpose (e.g. "red_all_400").
 *  - This JVD has ~600 VLANs per VRF; this template shows the repeating pattern.
 *  - l3-interface irb.<unit> enables symmetric IRB routing in the ERB model —
 *    present only on VLANs that need L3 gateway (routed VRFs).
 *  - VLANs without l3-interface are pure L2 segments (green VRF in this JVD).
 *  - Defined inside the mac-vrf routing instance's vlans block on Junos.
 *
 * Pair with:
 *  - junos/interfaces/irb-gateway.conf       (IRB units referenced by l3-interface)
 *  - junos/services/mac-vrf-evpn-vxlan.conf  (mac-vrf instance that owns these vlans)
 *  - junos/interfaces/trunk-access-port.conf (access ports carry these VLANs)
 *  - junos/interfaces/lag-esi-access.conf          (lag-esi-access)
 *  - junos/transport/evpn-vxlan-shared-tunnels.conf (evpn-vxlan-shared-tunnels)
 *
 * Variables (example values from esi-leaf1_qfx5120-48y):
 *   $VLAN_NAME       e.g. vn400
 *   $VLAN_DESC       e.g. red_all_400
 *   $VLAN_ID         e.g. 400
 *   $VNI_ID          e.g. 10400
 *   $L3_INTERFACE    e.g. irb.400  (omit if pure L2)
 */
vlans {
    $VLAN_NAME {
        description $VLAN_DESC;
        vlan-id $VLAN_ID;
        l3-interface $L3_INTERFACE;
        vxlan {
            vni $VNI_ID;
        }
    }
}
```

## junos/oam/ipv6-router-advertisement.conf

```
/*
 * Topic:   IPv6 Router Advertisement on IRB/WAN interfaces
 * Seen on:
 *   Junos: borderleaf1_qfx5120-32c borderleaf2_qfx5120-32c
 *   EVO:   borderleaf1_qfx5130-32cd borderleaf2_qfx5130-32cd
 *
 * Highlights:
 *  - Generated by Juniper Apstra — enables SLAAC/RA on external-facing interfaces
 *    for IPv6 neighbor discovery and prefix advertisement.
 *  - Tuned intervals (max 30s / min 10s) provide faster convergence than defaults.
 *  - Applied to WAN-facing VLAN sub-interfaces on border-leaf only.
 *
 * Pair with:
 *  - junos/interfaces/external-vlan-tagged.conf  (interfaces listed here)
 *  - junos/services/vrf-evpn-ip-prefix.conf      (VRF binds these interfaces)
 *  - junos/interfaces/irb-gateway.conf             (irb-gateway)
 *
 * Variables (example values from borderleaf1_qfx5130-32cd):
 *   $INTERFACE  e.g. et-0/0/8:0.299, et-0/0/8:0.399
 */
protocols {
    router-advertisement {
        interface $INTERFACE {
            max-advertisement-interval 30;
            min-advertisement-interval 10;
        }
        /* ... repeat per IPv6-enabled external interface ... */
    }
}
```

## junos/oam/l2-learning-telemetry.conf

```
/*
 * Topic:   L2 learning telemetry for remote MAC entry visibility
 * Seen on:
 *   Junos: esi-leaf1_qfx5120-48y esi-leaf2_qfx5120-48y server-leaf1_qfx5120-48y borderleaf1_qfx5120-32c borderleaf2_qfx5120-32c
 *   EVO:   spine1_qfx5220-32cd spine2_qfx5220-32cd borderleaf1_qfx5130-32cd borderleaf2_qfx5130-32cd
 *
 * Highlights:
 *  - Generated by Juniper Apstra — enables streaming of remote (EVPN-learned)
 *    MAC table entries to telemetry collectors for fabric-wide MAC visibility.
 *  - Single knob; no additional parameters required.
 *
 * Pair with: (standalone — no dependencies)
 */
protocols {
    l2-learning {
        telemetry {
            enable-remote-entries;
        }
    }
}
```

## junos/oam/lldp-discovery.conf

```
/*
 * Topic:   LLDP neighbor discovery configuration
 * Seen on:
 *   Junos: esi-leaf1_qfx5120-48y esi-leaf2_qfx5120-48y server-leaf1_qfx5120-48y borderleaf1_qfx5120-32c borderleaf2_qfx5120-32c
 *   EVO:   spine1_qfx5220-32cd spine2_qfx5220-32cd borderleaf1_qfx5130-32cd borderleaf2_qfx5130-32cd
 *
 * Highlights:
 *  - Generated by Juniper Apstra — enables LLDP on all interfaces for topology
 *    discovery and Apstra device-lifecycle management.
 *  - port-id-subtype interface-name ensures LLDP TLVs carry the logical interface
 *    name (not MAC), matching Apstra's wiring validation expectations.
 *  - neighbour-port-info-display port-id controls how neighbor info appears in
 *    "show lldp neighbors" output.
 *
 * Pair with: (standalone — no dependencies)
 */
protocols {
    lldp {
        port-id-subtype interface-name;
        port-description-type interface-description;
        neighbour-port-info-display port-id;
        interface all;
    }
}
```

## junos/oam/rstp-bridge.conf

```
/*
 * Topic:   RSTP bridge configuration with edge-port BPDU blocking
 * Seen on:
 *   Junos: esi-leaf1_qfx5120-48y esi-leaf2_qfx5120-48y server-leaf1_qfx5120-48y borderleaf1_qfx5120-32c borderleaf2_qfx5120-32c
 *   EVO:   borderleaf1_qfx5130-32cd borderleaf2_qfx5130-32cd
 *
 * Highlights:
 *  - Generated by Juniper Apstra — leaf/border-leaf runs RSTP as root bridge
 *    (bridge-priority 0) to maintain a predictable L2 topology.
 *  - bpdu-block-on-edge disables ports receiving unexpected BPDUs (server-facing
 *    ports should never send BPDUs; if they do, it indicates a loop or miscabling).
 *  - Spine has `protocols rstp { disable; }` (no L2 on spine in pure L3 CLOS).
 *
 * Pair with:
 *  - junos/interfaces/lag-esi-access.conf   (edge interfaces protected by BPDU block)
 *
 * Variables: none (leaf config is static)
 */
protocols {
    rstp {
        bridge-priority 0;
        bpdu-block-on-edge;
    }
}
```

## junos/oam/sflow-telemetry.conf

```
/*
 * Topic:   sFlow telemetry sampling and collector configuration
 * Seen on:
 *   Junos: esi-leaf1_qfx5120-48y esi-leaf2_qfx5120-48y server-leaf1_qfx5120-48y borderleaf1_qfx5120-32c borderleaf2_qfx5120-32c
 *   EVO:   spine1_qfx5220-32cd spine2_qfx5220-32cd borderleaf1_qfx5130-32cd borderleaf2_qfx5130-32cd
 *
 * Highlights:
 *  - Generated by Juniper Apstra — sFlow provides per-interface packet sampling
 *    for traffic visibility and capacity planning.
 *  - Sample-rate 1:128 ingress/egress with 20s polling balances telemetry
 *    granularity against CPU overhead.
 *  - source-ip should be a loopback or management IP reachable from the collector.
 *  - Interfaces list is typically all fabric-facing and access-facing ports.
 *
 * Pair with: (standalone — no dependencies)
 *
 * Variables (example values from borderleaf1_qfx5130-32cd):
 *   $SOURCE_IP       e.g. 10.48.9.240
 *   $COLLECTOR_IP    e.g. 10.87.84.51
 *   $COLLECTOR_PORT  e.g. 9995
 *   $INTERFACE       e.g. et-0/0/2.0
 */
protocols {
    sflow {
        polling-interval 20;
        sample-rate {
            ingress 128;
            egress 128;
        }
        source-ip $SOURCE_IP;
        collector $COLLECTOR_IP {
            udp-port $COLLECTOR_PORT;
        }
        interfaces $INTERFACE;
        /* ... repeat per monitored interface ... */
    }
}
```

## junos/policy/bgp-aos-export-policy.conf

```
/*
 * Topic:   BGP-AOS-Policy master export filter chaining AllPodNetworks and BGP redistribution
 * Seen on:
 *   Junos: esi-leaf1_qfx5120-48y esi-leaf2_qfx5120-48y server-leaf1_qfx5120-48y borderleaf1_qfx5120-32c borderleaf2_qfx5120-32c
 *   EVO:   spine1_qfx5220-32cd spine2_qfx5220-32cd borderleaf1_qfx5130-32cd borderleaf2_qfx5130-32cd
 *
 * Highlights:
 *  - Generated by Juniper Apstra — "BGP-AOS-Policy" is the Apstra master export
 *    policy applied to all fabric BGP sessions (both underlay and per-VRF).
 *  - Base policy accepts routes matching AllPodNetworks OR received via BGP; rejects all else.
 *  - Per-VRF variants (BGP-AOS-Policy-blue, -red) chain to AllPodNetworks-<vrf>
 *    and are applied to the VRF's EVPN ip-prefix-routes export.
 *  - Spine has only the base; leaf/border-leaf adds per-VRF variants.
 *
 * Pair with:
 *  - junos/policy/pod-network-export.conf  (AllPodNetworks policies referenced here)
 *  - junos/transport/bgp-underlay-fabric.conf  (fabric BGP applies this as export)
 *  - junos/services/vrf-evpn-ip-prefix.conf        (vrf-evpn-ip-prefix)
 *
 * Variables (example values from borderleaf1_qfx5130-32cd):
 *   $VRF_NAME     e.g. blue
 */
policy-options {
    policy-statement BGP-AOS-Policy {
        term BGP-AOS-Policy-10 {
            from policy AllPodNetworks;
            then accept;
        }
        term BGP-AOS-Policy-20 {
            from protocol bgp;
            then accept;
        }
        term BGP-AOS-Policy-100 {
            then reject;
        }
    }
    policy-statement BGP-AOS-Policy-$VRF_NAME {
        term BGP-AOS-Policy-$VRF_NAME-10 {
            from policy AllPodNetworks-$VRF_NAME;
            then accept;
        }
        term BGP-AOS-Policy-$VRF_NAME-20 {
            from protocol bgp;
            then accept;
        }
        term BGP-AOS-Policy-$VRF_NAME-100 {
            then reject;
        }
    }
}
```

## junos/policy/community-definitions.conf

```
/*
 * Topic:   BGP community definitions for fabric signaling and route tagging
 * Seen on:
 *   Junos: esi-leaf1_qfx5120-48y esi-leaf2_qfx5120-48y server-leaf1_qfx5120-48y borderleaf1_qfx5120-32c borderleaf2_qfx5120-32c
 *   EVO:   spine1_qfx5220-32cd spine2_qfx5220-32cd borderleaf1_qfx5130-32cd borderleaf2_qfx5130-32cd
 *
 * Highlights:
 *  - Generated by Juniper Apstra — community values encode fabric-tier origin,
 *    address-family, and VRF membership for route-leak and loop-prevention policies.
 *  - FABRIC_COMMUNITIES uses a wildcard regex match to bulk-delete all internal
 *    communities when exporting to external peers.
 *  - Spine devices carry only DEFAULT_DIRECT, FROM_SPINE_*, and FABRIC_COMMUNITIES.
 *  - Leaf/border-leaf adds per-VRF and RoutesFromExt communities.
 *
 * Pair with:
 *  - junos/policy/leaf-to-spine-evpn-filter.conf   (FROM_SPINE_EVPN_TIER)
 *  - junos/policy/leaf-to-spine-fabric-filter.conf  (FROM_SPINE_FABRIC_TIER)
 *  - junos/policy/external-route-export.conf        (FABRIC_COMMUNITIES delete)
 *  - junos/policy/external-route-import.conf        (RoutesFromExt communities)
 *  - junos/policy/pod-network-export.conf           (DEFAULT_DIRECT, VRF communities)
 *
 * Variables (example values from borderleaf1_qfx5130-32cd):
 *   $VRF_NAME          e.g. blue, red
 *   $VRF_COMM_V4       e.g. BLUE_COMMUNITY_V4
 *   $VRF_MEMBERS_V4    e.g. [ 3:20007 21003:26000 ]
 *   $VRF_COMM_V6       e.g. BLUE_COMMUNITY_V6
 *   $VRF_MEMBERS_V6    e.g. [ 3:20008 21003:26000 ]
 *   $EXT_PEER_NAME     e.g. external_router
 */
policy-options {
    /* Base communities — present on all devices */
    community DEFAULT_DIRECT_V4 members [ 3:20007 21001:26000 ];
    community DEFAULT_DIRECT_V6 members [ 3:20008 21001:26000 ];
    community FROM_SPINE_EVPN_TIER members 0:14;
    community FROM_SPINE_FABRIC_TIER members 0:15;
    community FABRIC_COMMUNITIES members [ 0:12 0:13 0:14 0:15 .+:200.. 2....:260.. ];

    /* Per-VRF communities — one pair (v4 + v6) per VRF on leaf/border-leaf */
    community $VRF_COMM_V4 members $VRF_MEMBERS_V4;
    community $VRF_COMM_V6 members $VRF_MEMBERS_V6;

    /* External-peer import communities — border-leaf only */
    community RoutesFromExt-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME members [ 3:20009 $VRF_ID:26000 ];
    community RoutesFromExtV6-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME members [ 3:20010 $VRF_ID:26000 ];
}
```

## junos/policy/external-route-export.conf

```
/*
 * Topic:   External route export policy with fabric community stripping
 * Seen on:
 *   Junos: borderleaf1_qfx5120-32c borderleaf2_qfx5120-32c
 *   EVO:   borderleaf1_qfx5130-32cd borderleaf2_qfx5130-32cd
 *
 * Highlights:
 *  - Generated by Juniper Apstra — applied as BGP export toward external peers.
 *  - Strips internal fabric communities (FABRIC_COMMUNITIES) before advertising
 *    routes externally — prevents internal topology leakage.
 *  - Uses route-filter-lists to control exactly which prefixes are exported per VRF.
 *  - Dual-stack: separate inet/inet6 terms with corresponding filter-lists.
 *
 * Pair with:
 *  - junos/policy/route-filter-lists.conf       (RoutesToExt filter-lists)
 *  - junos/policy/community-definitions.conf    (FABRIC_COMMUNITIES definition)
 *  - junos/services/vrf-evpn-ip-prefix.conf     (VRF BGP applies this as export)
 *
 * Variables (example values from borderleaf1_qfx5130-32cd):
 *   $VRF_NAME       e.g. blue
 *   $EXT_PEER_NAME  e.g. external_router
 */
policy-options {
    policy-statement RoutesToExt-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME {
        term RoutesToExt-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME-10 {
            from {
                family inet;
                route-filter-list RoutesToExt-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME;
            }
            then {
                community delete FABRIC_COMMUNITIES;
                accept;
            }
        }
        term RoutesToExt-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME-20 {
            from {
                family inet6;
                route-filter-list RoutesToExtV6-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME;
            }
            then {
                community delete FABRIC_COMMUNITIES;
                accept;
            }
        }
        term RoutesToExt-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME-30 {
            from family inet;
            then reject;
        }
        term RoutesToExt-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME-40 {
            from family inet6;
            then reject;
        }
    }
}
```

## junos/policy/external-route-import.conf

```
/*
 * Topic:   External route import policy with route-filter-list and community tagging
 * Seen on:
 *   Junos: borderleaf1_qfx5120-32c borderleaf2_qfx5120-32c
 *   EVO:   borderleaf1_qfx5130-32cd borderleaf2_qfx5130-32cd
 *
 * Highlights:
 *  - Generated by Juniper Apstra — applied as BGP import on external VRF peers.
 *  - Dual-stack: separate terms for inet (v4) and inet6 (v6) with corresponding
 *    route-filter-lists and community values.
 *  - Routes matching the filter-list are tagged with a per-VRF import community
 *    for fabric-wide route identification.
 *  - Final reject terms ensure only explicitly permitted prefixes enter the VRF.
 *
 * Pair with:
 *  - junos/policy/route-filter-lists.conf       (filter-lists referenced here)
 *  - junos/policy/community-definitions.conf    (import communities)
 *  - junos/services/vrf-evpn-ip-prefix.conf     (VRF BGP applies this as import)
 *
 * Variables (example values from borderleaf1_qfx5130-32cd):
 *   $VRF_NAME          e.g. blue
 *   $EXT_PEER_NAME     e.g. external_router
 *   $IMPORT_COMMUNITY  e.g. RoutesFromExt-blue-external_router
 *   $IMPORT_COMM_V6    e.g. RoutesFromExtV6-blue-external_router
 */
policy-options {
    policy-statement RoutesFromExt-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME {
        term RoutesFromExt-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME-10 {
            from {
                family inet;
                route-filter-list RoutesFromExt-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME;
            }
            then {
                community add $IMPORT_COMMUNITY;
                accept;
            }
        }
        term RoutesFromExt-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME-20 {
            from {
                family inet6;
                route-filter-list RoutesFromExtV6-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME;
            }
            then {
                community add $IMPORT_COMM_V6;
                accept;
            }
        }
        term RoutesFromExt-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME-30 {
            from family inet;
            then reject;
        }
        term RoutesFromExt-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME-40 {
            from family inet6;
            then reject;
        }
    }
}
```

## junos/policy/leaf-to-spine-evpn-filter.conf

```
/*
 * Topic:   LEAF_TO_SPINE_EVPN_OUT loop-prevention filter rejecting spine-originated EVPN routes
 * Seen on:
 *   Junos: esi-leaf1_qfx5120-48y esi-leaf2_qfx5120-48y server-leaf1_qfx5120-48y borderleaf1_qfx5120-32c borderleaf2_qfx5120-32c
 *   EVO:   borderleaf1_qfx5130-32cd borderleaf2_qfx5130-32cd
 *
 * Highlights:
 *  - Generated by Juniper Apstra — prevents EVPN routes learned from the spine tier
 *    from being re-advertised back to spines (loop prevention in eBGP CLOS).
 *  - Matches community FROM_SPINE_EVPN_TIER (0:14) set by the spine's
 *    SPINE_TO_LEAF_EVPN_OUT export policy.
 *  - Applied as export on the leaf's l3clos-l-evpn BGP group toward spines.
 *
 * Pair with:
 *  - junos/transport/bgp-evpn-overlay.conf   (applied as export policy)
 *  - junos/policy/community-definitions.conf (FROM_SPINE_EVPN_TIER community)
 */
policy-options {
    policy-statement LEAF_TO_SPINE_EVPN_OUT {
        term LEAF_TO_SPINE_EVPN_OUT-10 {
            from {
                protocol bgp;
                community FROM_SPINE_EVPN_TIER;
            }
            then reject;
        }
        term LEAF_TO_SPINE_EVPN_OUT-20 {
            then accept;
        }
    }
}
```

## junos/policy/leaf-to-spine-fabric-filter.conf

```
/*
 * Topic:   LEAF_TO_SPINE_FABRIC_OUT loop-prevention filter rejecting spine-originated fabric routes
 * Seen on:
 *   Junos: esi-leaf1_qfx5120-48y esi-leaf2_qfx5120-48y server-leaf1_qfx5120-48y borderleaf1_qfx5120-32c borderleaf2_qfx5120-32c
 *   EVO:   borderleaf1_qfx5130-32cd borderleaf2_qfx5130-32cd
 *
 * Highlights:
 *  - Generated by Juniper Apstra — prevents IPv4 unicast fabric routes learned from
 *    spines from being re-advertised back (underlay loop prevention).
 *  - Matches community FROM_SPINE_FABRIC_TIER (0:15) set by spine's
 *    SPINE_TO_LEAF_FABRIC_OUT export.
 *  - Applied as export on the leaf's l3clos-l BGP group toward spines.
 *
 * Pair with:
 *  - junos/transport/bgp-underlay-fabric.conf  (applied as export policy)
 *  - junos/policy/community-definitions.conf   (FROM_SPINE_FABRIC_TIER community)
 */
policy-options {
    policy-statement LEAF_TO_SPINE_FABRIC_OUT {
        term LEAF_TO_SPINE_FABRIC_OUT-10 {
            from {
                protocol bgp;
                community FROM_SPINE_FABRIC_TIER;
            }
            then reject;
        }
        term LEAF_TO_SPINE_FABRIC_OUT-20 {
            then accept;
        }
    }
}
```

## junos/policy/per-packet-load-balance.conf

```
/*
 * Topic:   Per-packet load-balance policy for PFE forwarding-table export
 * Seen on:
 *   Junos: esi-leaf1_qfx5120-48y esi-leaf2_qfx5120-48y server-leaf1_qfx5120-48y borderleaf1_qfx5120-32c borderleaf2_qfx5120-32c
 *   EVO:   spine1_qfx5220-32cd spine2_qfx5220-32cd borderleaf1_qfx5130-32cd borderleaf2_qfx5130-32cd
 *
 * Highlights:
 *  - Generated by Juniper Apstra — identical on all devices regardless of role or OS.
 *  - "per-packet" is a misnomer carried from early Junos — it actually means per-flow
 *    hash-based ECMP (not true per-packet round-robin).
 *  - Referenced by routing-options forwarding-table export on every device.
 *
 * Pair with:
 *  - junos/transport/forwarding-table-ecmp.conf  (references this policy)
 */
policy-options {
    policy-statement PFE-LB {
        then {
            load-balance per-packet;
        }
    }
}
```

## junos/policy/pod-network-export.conf

```
/*
 * Topic:   AllPodNetworks direct-route export policy with per-VRF community tagging
 * Seen on:
 *   Junos: esi-leaf1_qfx5120-48y esi-leaf2_qfx5120-48y server-leaf1_qfx5120-48y borderleaf1_qfx5120-32c borderleaf2_qfx5120-32c
 *   EVO:   spine1_qfx5220-32cd spine2_qfx5220-32cd borderleaf1_qfx5130-32cd borderleaf2_qfx5130-32cd
 *
 * Highlights:
 *  - Generated by Juniper Apstra — "AllPodNetworks" is the Apstra convention for
 *    tagging directly-connected subnets with community values for fabric-wide filtering.
 *  - Base policy (AllPodNetworks) tags all direct routes with DEFAULT_DIRECT communities.
 *  - Per-VRF variants (AllPodNetworks-blue, AllPodNetworks-red) add VRF-specific
 *    community values — used by EVPN ip-prefix-routes export on leaf nodes.
 *  - Spine has only the base policy; leafs/border-leafs add per-VRF variants.
 *  - Term ordering: inet (v4) → inet6 (v6) → reject-all (safety net).
 *
 * Pair with:
 *  - junos/policy/bgp-aos-export-policy.conf  (chains from-policy AllPodNetworks)
 *  - junos/policy/community-definitions.conf  (community values referenced here)
 *
 * Variables (example values from esi-leaf1_qfx5120-48y):
 *   $VRF_NAME          e.g. blue
 *   $VRF_COMMUNITY_V4  e.g. BLUE_COMMUNITY_V4
 *   $VRF_COMMUNITY_V6  e.g. BLUE_COMMUNITY_V6
 */
policy-options {
    policy-statement AllPodNetworks {
        term AllPodNetworks-10 {
            from {
                family inet;
                protocol direct;
            }
            then {
                community add DEFAULT_DIRECT_V4;
                accept;
            }
        }
        term AllPodNetworks-20 {
            from {
                family inet6;
                protocol direct;
            }
            then {
                community add DEFAULT_DIRECT_V6;
                accept;
            }
        }
        term AllPodNetworks-100 {
            then reject;
        }
    }
    policy-statement AllPodNetworks-$VRF_NAME {
        term AllPodNetworks-$VRF_NAME-10 {
            from {
                family inet;
                protocol direct;
            }
            then {
                community add $VRF_COMMUNITY_V4;
                accept;
            }
        }
        term AllPodNetworks-$VRF_NAME-20 {
            from {
                family inet6;
                protocol direct;
            }
            then {
                community add $VRF_COMMUNITY_V6;
                accept;
            }
        }
        term AllPodNetworks-$VRF_NAME-100 {
            then reject;
        }
    }
}
```

## junos/policy/route-filter-lists.conf

```
/*
 * Topic:   Route-filter-list definitions for external peer import/export control
 * Seen on:
 *   Junos: borderleaf1_qfx5120-32c borderleaf2_qfx5120-32c
 *   EVO:   borderleaf1_qfx5130-32cd borderleaf2_qfx5130-32cd
 *
 * Highlights:
 *  - Generated by Juniper Apstra — one set of filter-lists per VRF per external peer.
 *  - RoutesFromExt: simple default-route match (0.0.0.0/0 exact) — accepts only
 *    a default from the external peer.
 *  - RoutesToExt: detailed prefix ranges defining which internal subnets are
 *    advertised externally; values are site-specific.
 *  - IPv6 variants mirror the IPv4 logic with 2001:db8: documentation prefixes.
 *  - Border-leaf only; leaf/spine devices have no route-filter-lists.
 *
 * Pair with:
 *  - junos/policy/external-route-import.conf  (references RoutesFromExt lists)
 *  - junos/policy/external-route-export.conf  (references RoutesToExt lists)
 *
 * Variables (example values from borderleaf1_qfx5130-32cd):
 *   $VRF_NAME       e.g. blue
 *   $EXT_PEER_NAME  e.g. external_router
 *   $PREFIX/$LEN    site-specific prefix entries
 */
policy-options {
    /* Import filter — accept only default route from external peer */
    route-filter-list RoutesFromExt-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME {
        0.0.0.0/0 exact;
    }
    route-filter-list RoutesFromExtV6-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME {
        0::0/0 exact;
    }

    /* Export filter — advertise specific internal prefixes to external peer */
    route-filter-list RoutesToExt-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME {
        0.0.0.0/0 prefix-length-range /1-/32;
        $PREFIX/$LEN upto /32;
        /* ... additional site-specific entries ... */
    }
    route-filter-list RoutesToExtV6-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME {
        ::/0 prefix-length-range /1-/127;
        $PREFIX6/$LEN6 upto /128;
        /* ... additional site-specific entries ... */
    }
}
```

## junos/services/mac-vrf-evpn-vxlan.conf

```
/*
 * Topic:   MAC-VRF instance with EVPN/VXLAN encapsulation and per-VNI route-targets
 * Seen on:
 *   Junos: esi-leaf1_qfx5120-48y esi-leaf2_qfx5120-48y server-leaf1_qfx5120-48y
 *
 * Highlights:
 *  - Generated by Juniper Apstra — service-type vlan-aware mac-vrf aggregates all
 *    L2 VNIs into a single EVPN instance with per-VNI vrf-target for fine-grained
 *    import/export control.
 *  - default-gateway do-not-advertise suppresses Type-2 MAC/IP for anycast gateways
 *    (expected with ERB — each leaf owns its own gateway MAC locally).
 *  - duplicate-mac-detection with auto-recovery-time 9 handles loop/miscabling.
 *  - extended-vni-list "all" enables all VNIs without explicit enumeration on the
 *    forwarding plane.
 *  - vlans stanza maps VLAN-ID → VNI and binds the L3 IRB interface for routed traffic.
 *  - Junos-only pattern — EVO border-leaf devices use a different EVPN model.
 *
 * Pair with:
 *  - junos/interfaces/vlan-vxlan-domain.conf   (VLAN→VNI in [vlans] hierarchy)
 *  - junos/interfaces/irb-gateway.conf         (IRB interfaces referenced in vlans)
 *  - junos/interfaces/lag-esi-access.conf      (access interfaces bound here)
 *  - junos/transport/evpn-vxlan-shared-tunnels.conf (forwarding-options vxlan-routing)
 *
 *
 * JVD service mapping:
 *   1 instances total (high 1 / med 0 / low 0)
 *   On devices: esi-leaf1_qfx5120-48y (1), esi-leaf2_qfx5120-48y (1), server-leaf1_qfx5120-48y (1)
 *   Example: evpn-1 (RD 192.168.255.4:65534, RT target:10400:1)
 *     esi-leaf1_qfx5120-48y  xe-0/0/0.0
 *     esi-leaf2_qfx5120-48y  xe-0/0/0.0
 *     server-leaf1_qfx5120-48y  xe-0/0/0.0
 *
 * Variables (example values from esi-leaf1_qfx5120-48y):
 *   $INSTANCE_NAME      e.g. evpn-1
 *   $VNI                e.g. 10400
 *   $VNI_TARGET         e.g. target:10400:1
 *   $VTEP_SOURCE        e.g. lo0.0
 *   $RD                 e.g. 192.168.255.4:65534
 *   $GLOBAL_VRF_TARGET  e.g. target:100:100
 *   $VLAN_NAME          e.g. vn1400
 *   $VLAN_DESC          e.g. blue_all_1400
 *   $VLAN_ID            e.g. 1400
 *   $IRB_UNIT           e.g. 1400
 *   $VXLAN_VNI          e.g. 11400
 *   $ACCESS_INTERFACE   e.g. ae1.0
 *   $AUTO_RECOVERY_TIME e.g. 9
 */
routing-instances {
    $INSTANCE_NAME {
        instance-type mac-vrf;
        protocols {
            evpn {
                encapsulation vxlan;
                default-gateway do-not-advertise;
                duplicate-mac-detection {
                    auto-recovery-time $AUTO_RECOVERY_TIME;
                }
                extended-vni-list all;
                vni-options {
                    vni $VNI {
                        vrf-target $VNI_TARGET;
                    }
                    /* ... repeat per VNI ... */
                }
            }
        }
        vtep-source-interface $VTEP_SOURCE;
        service-type vlan-aware;
        interface $ACCESS_INTERFACE;
        /* ... repeat per access interface ... */
        route-distinguisher $RD;
        vrf-target $GLOBAL_VRF_TARGET;
        vlans {
            $VLAN_NAME {
                description $VLAN_DESC;
                vlan-id $VLAN_ID;
                l3-interface irb.$IRB_UNIT;
                vxlan {
                    vni $VXLAN_VNI;
                }
            }
            /* ... repeat per VLAN ... */
        }
    }
}
```

## junos/services/vrf-evpn-ip-prefix.conf

```
/*
 * Topic:   VRF instance with EVPN ip-prefix-routes, optional eBGP external peering, and DHCP relay
 * Seen on:
 *   Junos: esi-leaf1_qfx5120-48y esi-leaf2_qfx5120-48y server-leaf1_qfx5120-48y borderleaf1_qfx5120-32c borderleaf2_qfx5120-32c
 *   EVO:   borderleaf1_qfx5130-32cd borderleaf2_qfx5130-32cd
 *
 * Highlights:
 *  - Generated by Juniper Apstra — instance-type vrf with EVPN Type-5 ip-prefix-routes.
 *  - Border-leaf variant includes protocols bgp group "l3rtr" for eBGP peering
 *    to external routers (dual-stack, import/export policies per peer).
 *  - Leaf variant omits BGP — only EVPN + DHCP relay + interface list.
 *  - DHCP relay uses forward-only with relay-option-82 server-id-override.
 *  - route-distinguisher and vrf-target are auto-assigned by Apstra.
 *
 * Pair with:
 *  - junos/policy/bgp-aos-export-policy.conf     (BGP-AOS-Policy-$VRF_NAME on evpn export)
 *  - junos/policy/external-route-import.conf      (import on l3rtr neighbors)
 *  - junos/policy/external-route-export.conf      (export on l3rtr neighbors)
 *  - junos/interfaces/irb-gateway.conf            (IRB interfaces bound here)
 *  - junos/interfaces/external-vlan-tagged.conf    (external-vlan-tagged)
 *  - junos/interfaces/loopback-multi-unit.conf     (loopback-multi-unit)
 *  - junos/oam/ipv6-router-advertisement.conf      (ipv6-router-advertisement)
 *
 *
 * JVD service mapping:
 *   3 instances total (high 3 / med 0 / low 0)
 *   On devices: esi-leaf1_qfx5120-48y (3), esi-leaf2_qfx5120-48y (3), server-leaf1_qfx5120-48y (3)
 *   Example: blue (RD 192.168.255.4:3, RT target:20002:1)
 *     esi-leaf1_qfx5120-48y
 *     esi-leaf2_qfx5120-48y
 *     server-leaf1_qfx5120-48y
 *
 * Variables (example values from borderleaf1_qfx5130-32cd VRF "blue"):
 *   $VRF_NAME          e.g. blue
 *   $VNI               e.g. 20002
 *   $RD                e.g. 192.168.255.2:3
 *   $VRF_TARGET        e.g. target:20002:1
 *   $LO_UNIT           e.g. 3
 *   $DHCP_SERVER       e.g. 10.10.0.200
 *   $EXT_NEIGHBOR_V4   e.g. 10.200.0.5
 *   $EXT_LOCAL_V4      e.g. 10.200.0.4
 *   $EXT_NEIGHBOR_V6   e.g. 2001:db8:dc1:10:200::5
 *   $EXT_LOCAL_V6      e.g. 2001:db8:dc1:10:200::4
 *   $EXT_PEER_AS       e.g. 65000
 *   $EXT_PEER_NAME     e.g. external_router
 *   $WAN_INTERFACE     e.g. et-0/0/8:0.399
 */
routing-instances {
    $VRF_NAME {
        instance-type vrf;
        routing-options {
            rib $VRF_NAME.inet6.0 {
                multipath;
            }
            graceful-restart;
            multipath;
            auto-export;
        }
        protocols {
            /* Border-leaf only: eBGP to external router */
            bgp {
                group l3rtr {
                    type external;
                    multihop {
                        ttl 1;
                    }
                    family inet {
                        unicast {
                            loops 2;
                        }
                    }
                    family inet6 {
                        unicast {
                            loops 2;
                        }
                    }
                    multipath {
                        multiple-as;
                    }
                    neighbor $EXT_NEIGHBOR_V6 {
                        description facing_$EXT_PEER_NAME;
                        multihop {
                            ttl 2;
                        }
                        local-address $EXT_LOCAL_V6;
                        import ( RoutesFromExt-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME );
                        family inet6 {
                            unicast;
                        }
                        export ( RoutesToExt-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME );
                        peer-as $EXT_PEER_AS;
                    }
                    neighbor $EXT_NEIGHBOR_V4 {
                        description facing_$EXT_PEER_NAME;
                        multihop {
                            ttl 2;
                        }
                        local-address $EXT_LOCAL_V4;
                        import ( RoutesFromExt-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME );
                        family inet {
                            unicast;
                        }
                        export ( RoutesToExt-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME );
                        peer-as $EXT_PEER_AS;
                    }
                }
                graceful-restart {
                    dont-help-shared-fate-bfd-down;
                }
            }
            evpn {
                ip-prefix-routes {
                    advertise direct-nexthop;
                    encapsulation vxlan;
                    vni $VNI;
                    export BGP-AOS-Policy-$VRF_NAME;
                }
            }
        }
        forwarding-options {
            dhcp-relay {
                forward-only;
                server-group {
                    $VRF_NAME {
                        $DHCP_SERVER;
                    }
                }
                group $VRF_NAME {
                    active-server-group $VRF_NAME;
                    relay-option-82 {
                        server-id-override;
                    }
                }
            }
        }
        interface $WAN_INTERFACE;
        interface lo0.$LO_UNIT;
        route-distinguisher $RD;
        vrf-target $VRF_TARGET;
    }
}
```

## junos/transport/bgp-evpn-overlay.conf

```
/*
 * Topic:   eBGP EVPN overlay peer group with multihop and signaling
 * Seen on:
 *   Junos: esi-leaf1_qfx5120-48y esi-leaf2_qfx5120-48y server-leaf1_qfx5120-48y borderleaf1_qfx5120-32c borderleaf2_qfx5120-32c
 *   EVO:   (none)
 *
 * Highlights:
 *  - Body is byte-identical to the EVO sibling.
 *  - Generated by Juniper Apstra — group name "l3clos-l-evpn" for leaf.
 *  - Overlay eBGP sessions use loopback addresses (multihop ttl 1), decoupled from
 *    physical link state.
 *  - no-nexthop-change preserves originating VTEP address through spine.
 *  - family evpn signaling loops 2 for inter-VRF re-entry via border leaf.
 *
 * Pair with:
 *  - junos/transport/bgp-underlay-fabric.conf      (underlay provides reachability)
 *  - junos/interfaces/loopback-multi-unit.conf     (lo0.0 as local-address)
 *  - junos/policy/leaf-to-spine-evpn-filter.conf   (export policy)
 *
 * Variables (example values from esi-leaf1_qfx5120-48y):
 *   $EVPN_GROUP        e.g. l3clos-l-evpn
 *   $OVERLAY_PEER      e.g. 192.168.255.0
 *   $OVERLAY_LOCAL     e.g. 192.168.255.4
 *   $PEER_DESCRIPTION  e.g. facing_spine1-evpn-overlay
 *   $PEER_AS           e.g. 64512
 *   $BFD_INTERVAL_MS   e.g. 3000
 *   $BFD_MULTIPLIER    e.g. 3
 */
protocols {
    bgp {
        group $EVPN_GROUP {
            type external;
            multihop {
                ttl 1;
                no-nexthop-change;
            }
            family evpn {
                signaling {
                    loops 2;
                }
            }
            multipath {
                multiple-as;
            }
            bfd-liveness-detection {
                minimum-interval $BFD_INTERVAL_MS;
                multiplier $BFD_MULTIPLIER;
            }
            neighbor $OVERLAY_PEER {
                description $PEER_DESCRIPTION;
                local-address $OVERLAY_LOCAL;
                family evpn {
                    signaling;
                }
                export ( LEAF_TO_SPINE_EVPN_OUT && EVPN_EXPORT );
                peer-as $PEER_AS;
            }
            vpn-apply-export;
        }
    }
}
```

## junos/transport/bgp-underlay-fabric.conf

```
/*
 * Topic:   eBGP underlay fabric peer group (leaf-to-spine or spine-to-leaf)
 * Seen on:
 *   Junos: esi-leaf1_qfx5120-48y esi-leaf2_qfx5120-48y server-leaf1_qfx5120-48y borderleaf1_qfx5120-32c borderleaf2_qfx5120-32c
 *   EVO:   (none)
 *
 * Highlights:
 *  - Body is byte-identical to the EVO sibling.
 *  - Generated by Juniper Apstra — group name "l3clos-l" for leaf, "l3clos-s" for spine.
 *  - eBGP with unique ASN per device; multipath multiple-as enables ECMP across spines.
 *  - BFD at 1000ms×3 for sub-second fabric convergence.
 *  - vpn-apply-export pushes EVPN routes through the fabric export chain.
 *
 * Pair with:
 *  - junos/transport/bgp-evpn-overlay.conf         (overlay BGP uses same spine peers)
 *  - junos/policy/leaf-to-spine-fabric-filter.conf  (export policy applied here)
 *  - junos/policy/bgp-aos-export-policy.conf        (chained export policy)
 *  - junos/interfaces/fabric-uplink.conf            (physical links)
 *  - junos/transport/forwarding-table-ecmp.conf    (forwarding-table-ecmp)
 *
 * Variables (example values from esi-leaf1_qfx5120-48y):
 *   $BGP_GROUP         e.g. l3clos-l
 *   $PEER_IP           e.g. 10.0.1.4
 *   $LOCAL_IP          e.g. 10.0.1.5
 *   $PEER_DESCRIPTION  e.g. facing_spine1
 *   $PEER_AS           e.g. 64512
 *   $BFD_INTERVAL_MS   e.g. 1000
 *   $BFD_MULTIPLIER    e.g. 3
 */
protocols {
    bgp {
        group $BGP_GROUP {
            type external;
            multipath {
                multiple-as;
            }
            bfd-liveness-detection {
                minimum-interval $BFD_INTERVAL_MS;
                multiplier $BFD_MULTIPLIER;
            }
            neighbor $PEER_IP {
                description $PEER_DESCRIPTION;
                local-address $LOCAL_IP;
                family inet {
                    unicast;
                }
                export ( LEAF_TO_SPINE_FABRIC_OUT && BGP-AOS-Policy );
                peer-as $PEER_AS;
            }
            vpn-apply-export;
        }
        log-updown;
        graceful-restart {
            dont-help-shared-fate-bfd-down;
        }
        multipath;
    }
}
```

## junos/transport/evpn-vxlan-shared-tunnels.conf

```
/*
 * Topic:   EVPN-VXLAN shared tunnels and VXLAN routing parameters
 * Seen on:
 *   Junos: esi-leaf1_qfx5120-48y esi-leaf2_qfx5120-48y server-leaf1_qfx5120-48y borderleaf1_qfx5120-32c borderleaf2_qfx5120-32c
 *   EVO:   (none)
 *
 * Highlights:
 *  - Generated by Juniper Apstra — these forwarding-options are Junos-specific;
 *    EVO platforms handle VXLAN tunnel sharing implicitly.
 *  - shared-tunnels: multiple VNIs share the same VXLAN tunnel to each remote VTEP,
 *    reducing the number of next-hops in the forwarding table.
 *  - vxlan-routing next-hop 45056: pre-allocates next-hop table entries for
 *    symmetric IRB routing across VNIs.
 *  - interface-num 8192: maximum number of VXLAN logical interfaces (scales with VNI count).
 *  - overlay-ecmp: enables ECMP across multiple VXLAN paths to the same destination.
 *
 * Pair with:
 *  - junos/transport/forwarding-table-ecmp.conf  (forwarding-table ECMP settings)
 *  - junos/interfaces/vlan-vxlan-domain.conf     (VLANs mapped to VNIs)
 *  - junos/services/mac-vrf-evpn-vxlan.conf      (mac-vrf instance using these tunnels)
 *
 * Variables (example values from esi-leaf1_qfx5120-48y):
 *   $VXLAN_NEXT_HOP     e.g. 45056
 *   $VXLAN_INTF_NUM     e.g. 8192
 */
forwarding-options {
    evpn-vxlan {
        shared-tunnels;
    }
    vxlan-routing {
        next-hop $VXLAN_NEXT_HOP;
        interface-num $VXLAN_INTF_NUM;
        overlay-ecmp;
    }
}
```

## junos/transport/forwarding-table-ecmp.conf

```
/*
 * Topic:   Forwarding-table ECMP with per-packet load-balance and chained-composite-next-hop
 * Seen on:
 *   Junos: esi-leaf1_qfx5120-48y esi-leaf2_qfx5120-48y server-leaf1_qfx5120-48y borderleaf1_qfx5120-32c borderleaf2_qfx5120-32c
 *   EVO:   (none)
 *
 * Highlights:
 *  - Body is byte-identical to the EVO sibling.
 *  - Generated by Juniper Apstra — standard ECMP tuning for EVPN/VXLAN fabrics.
 *  - export PFE-LB applies per-packet load-balance to all forwarding lookups.
 *  - ecmp-fast-reroute pre-programs backup paths for sub-second spine failover.
 *  - chained-composite-next-hop ingress evpn: required for EVPN Type-5 symmetric
 *    routing on leaf/border-leaf nodes.
 *
 * Pair with:
 *  - junos/policy/per-packet-load-balance.conf  (PFE-LB policy)
 *  - junos/transport/bgp-underlay-fabric.conf   (ECMP paths across spines)
 *  - junos/transport/evpn-vxlan-shared-tunnels.conf  (VXLAN tunnel parameters)
 *
 * Variables (example values from esi-leaf1_qfx5120-48y):
 *   $ROUTER_ID     e.g. 192.168.255.4
 *   $LOCAL_AS      e.g. 64516
 */
routing-options {
    router-id $ROUTER_ID;
    autonomous-system $LOCAL_AS;
    graceful-restart;
    forwarding-table {
        export PFE-LB;
        ecmp-fast-reroute;
        chained-composite-next-hop {
            ingress {
                evpn;
            }
        }
    }
}
```

## byoai/TIERS.md

# Configuration Form Tiers

This file is part of the [BYOAI](README.md) corpus. It tells the AI which snippet files to include for each service kind at each verbosity tier. It is bundled into [`jvd-3stage-dc-snips.md`](jvd-3stage-dc-snips.md) by `regenerate-bundle.sh`.

For each service kind, the AI includes ONLY the snips listed for the chosen tier — and ONLY those — unless the user explicitly asks for more. OS-select each file under `junos/` or `evo/` to match the target device family (spines + EVO border-leaves = EVO; leaves + Junos border-leaves = Junos).

The three tiers:

- **minimum** — just the new service instance + its attachment interface(s) + service-essential helpers (IRB, VLAN→VNI mapping). Assumes a working EVPN-VXLAN fabric (underlay eBGP + EVPN overlay + policies) is already present.
- **with-overlay** — `minimum` + the EVPN overlay peering (`bgp-evpn-overlay`) so the EVPN address family is re-asserted. Best when you're not sure the overlay is active.
- **as-deployed** — full JVD fabric baseline: service + underlay eBGP + EVPN overlay + fabric policies + forwarding-table ECMP + loopback + fabric uplinks + chassis + OAM. Best for a greenfield leaf/border-leaf turn-up or a complete working example.

---

## Shared fabric baseline (referenced by every `as-deployed`)

The EVPN-VXLAN Clos underlay + overlay that every leaf, border-leaf, and spine builds on. OS-select each file (spines and EVO border-leaves use `evo/`; leaves and Junos border-leaves use `junos/`):

- `bootstrap/chassis-port-config.conf` — chassis port-speed / aggregated-devices / FPC
- `bootstrap/grpc-certificate.conf` — gRPC TLS cert for Apstra telemetry
- `interfaces/fabric-uplink.conf` — point-to-point /31 spine ↔ leaf fabric links
- `interfaces/loopback-multi-unit.conf` — lo0 router-id + EVPN overlay + per-VRF units
- `transport/bgp-underlay-fabric.conf` — eBGP underlay (leaf ↔ spine)
- `transport/bgp-evpn-overlay.conf` — eBGP EVPN overlay peer group
- `transport/forwarding-table-ecmp.conf` — ECMP + per-packet load-balance export
- `policy/community-definitions.conf` — fabric + VRF community definitions
- `policy/pod-network-export.conf` — AllPodNetworks direct-route export
- `policy/bgp-aos-export-policy.conf` — master export filter (chains AllPodNetworks + redistribution)
- `policy/leaf-to-spine-fabric-filter.conf` — underlay loop-prevention export filter
- `policy/leaf-to-spine-evpn-filter.conf` — overlay loop-prevention export filter
- `policy/per-packet-load-balance.conf` — PFE load-balance policy (referenced by forwarding-table)
- `oam/lldp-discovery.conf` — LLDP neighbor discovery
- `oam/sflow-telemetry.conf` — sFlow sampling + collector
- `oam/l2-learning-telemetry.conf` — remote-MAC telemetry

> **Junos-only transport add:** `junos/transport/evpn-vxlan-shared-tunnels.conf` (shared VTEP tunnels + VXLAN routing) is part of the Junos leaf baseline whenever the L2 MAC-VRF service is present.

---

## mac-vrf-evpn-vxlan (L2 EVPN-VXLAN MAC-VRF)  ·  Junos only

Runs on the Junos server-facing leaves (`esi-leaf1/2`, `server-leaf1`). A vlan-aware MAC-VRF that aggregates all L2 VNIs into one EVPN instance with per-VNI route-targets.

**minimum** (just the service)
- `junos/services/mac-vrf-evpn-vxlan.conf`
- `junos/interfaces/vlan-vxlan-domain.conf` (VLAN → VNI mapping + L3 IRB binding)
- `junos/interfaces/irb-gateway.conf` (anycast IRB gateway referenced by the vlans)
- `junos/interfaces/lag-esi-access.conf` (ESI all-active LAG to multi-homed servers)
- `junos/interfaces/trunk-access-port.conf` (single-homed L2 trunk to servers)
- `junos/transport/evpn-vxlan-shared-tunnels.conf` (forwarding-options vxlan-routing)

**with-overlay** (= minimum +)
- `junos/transport/bgp-evpn-overlay.conf` (asserts the EVPN family toward the spines)
- `junos/oam/rstp-bridge.conf` (edge-port BPDU protection on access LAGs)

**as-deployed** (= with-overlay + the shared fabric baseline above)

---

## vrf-evpn-ip-prefix (L3 EVPN Type-5 VRF)  ·  Junos + EVO

Runs on the leaves (`esi-leaf1/2`, `server-leaf1`) and border-leaves (Junos `borderleaf1/2_qfx5120-32c`, EVO `borderleaf1/2_qfx5130-32cd`). A VRF with EVPN ip-prefix (Type-5) routes; the border-leaf variant additionally runs eBGP to an external router with DHCP relay.

**minimum** (just the service) — OS-select every file
- `services/vrf-evpn-ip-prefix.conf`
- `interfaces/irb-gateway.conf` (IRB interfaces bound to the VRF)
- `interfaces/loopback-multi-unit.conf` (per-VRF lo0 unit for the RD)
- `policy/bgp-aos-export-policy.conf` (`BGP-AOS-Policy-$VRF_NAME` on EVPN export)
- `oam/ipv6-router-advertisement.conf` (IPv6 RA on IRB/WAN units)

**with-overlay** (= minimum +)
- `transport/bgp-evpn-overlay.conf` (asserts the EVPN family)

**as-deployed** (= with-overlay + shared fabric baseline)

> **Border-leaf external-peering add** (when the VRF exits to a WAN / external router — `borderleaf*` only): also include
> - `interfaces/external-vlan-tagged.conf` (flexible-VLAN external breakout)
> - `policy/external-route-import.conf` + `policy/external-route-export.conf` (per-peer import/export)
> - `policy/route-filter-lists.conf` (RoutesFromExt / RoutesToExt prefix lists)

---

## Add-a-feature requests (no full service)

When the user asks to add a supporting feature to an existing device, emit ONLY that snip set (OS-select):
- **LLDP** → `oam/lldp-discovery.conf`
- **sFlow telemetry** → `oam/sflow-telemetry.conf`
- **L2 learning telemetry** → `oam/l2-learning-telemetry.conf`
- **IPv6 Router Advertisement** → `oam/ipv6-router-advertisement.conf`
- **RSTP / BPDU protection** → `junos/oam/rstp-bridge.conf`
- **ECMP / load-balancing** → `transport/forwarding-table-ecmp.conf` + `policy/per-packet-load-balance.conf`
- **ESI multihomed access LAG** → `junos/interfaces/lag-esi-access.conf` + `junos/bootstrap/chassis-port-config.conf`
- **Single-homed access trunk** → `junos/interfaces/trunk-access-port.conf` + `junos/interfaces/vlan-vxlan-domain.conf`
- **External / WAN exit** → `interfaces/external-vlan-tagged.conf` + `policy/external-route-import.conf` + `policy/external-route-export.conf` + `policy/route-filter-lists.conf`
- **gRPC / Apstra telemetry cert** → `bootstrap/grpc-certificate.conf`

## byoai/DEFAULTS.md

# Auto-Fill Defaults

This file is part of the [BYOAI](README.md) corpus. It gives the deterministic JVD lab-default values the AI uses in `auto` mode (or when the user short-circuits with `all defaults` / `use defaults` / `skip`). It is bundled into [`jvd-3stage-dc-snips.md`](jvd-3stage-dc-snips.md) by `regenerate-bundle.sh`.

Use these values EXACTLY. Do not invent alternative defaults. Every value the AI auto-fills MUST be listed in the output's `Inputs used:` block so the user can rerun with edits.

Addresses below are the JVD lab's actual values (from each device `.conf`, generated by Juniper Apstra). Substitute site values when deploying.

---

## Device inventory (the JVD topology — 3-stage Clos)

| Device | Platform | OS family | Role | Loopback (router-id) |
|--------|----------|-----------|------|----------------------|
| `spine1_qfx5220-32cd` | QFX5220-32CD | **EVO** | Spine — underlay + EVPN overlay peer (no services) | `192.168.255.0` |
| `spine2_qfx5220-32cd` | QFX5220-32CD | **EVO** | Spine — underlay + EVPN overlay peer (no services) | `192.168.255.1` |
| `esi-leaf1_qfx5120-48y` | QFX5120-48Y | Junos | Leaf — ESI multihomed server access, L2 MAC-VRF + L3 VRF | `192.168.255.4` |
| `esi-leaf2_qfx5120-48y` | QFX5120-48Y | Junos | Leaf — ESI multihomed server access, L2 MAC-VRF + L3 VRF | `192.168.255.5` |
| `server-leaf1_qfx5120-48y` | QFX5120-48Y | Junos | Leaf — single-homed server access, L2 MAC-VRF + L3 VRF | `192.168.255.6` |
| `borderleaf1_qfx5120-32c` | QFX5120-32C | Junos | Border-leaf — L3 VRF + eBGP external / WAN exit | `192.168.255.2` |
| `borderleaf2_qfx5120-32c` | QFX5120-32C | Junos | Border-leaf — L3 VRF + eBGP external / WAN exit | `192.168.255.3` |
| `borderleaf1_qfx5130-32cd` | QFX5130-32CD | **EVO** | Border-leaf — L3 VRF + eBGP external / WAN exit | `192.168.255.2` |
| `borderleaf2_qfx5130-32cd` | QFX5130-32CD | **EVO** | Border-leaf — L3 VRF + eBGP external / WAN exit | `192.168.255.3` |

> The Junos `borderleaf*_qfx5120-32c` and EVO `borderleaf*_qfx5130-32cd` are **hardware variants of the same border-leaf role** (the JVD validated both platforms). Pick the OS/platform the user wants — never mix a Junos and EVO border-leaf into the same generated pair.

**Device-choice shortcuts** (offered in the clarifying question):
- `SPINE` → `spine1_qfx5220-32cd` + `spine2_qfx5220-32cd` (EVO; underlay + EVPN overlay only — no service instances)
- `LEAF` → `esi-leaf1_qfx5120-48y` + `esi-leaf2_qfx5120-48y` + `server-leaf1_qfx5120-48y` (Junos; L2 MAC-VRF + L3 VRF)
- `BORDERLEAF-JUNOS` → `borderleaf1_qfx5120-32c` + `borderleaf2_qfx5120-32c` (Junos; L3 VRF + external peering)
- `BORDERLEAF-EVO` → `borderleaf1_qfx5130-32cd` + `borderleaf2_qfx5130-32cd` (EVO; L3 VRF + external peering)

The **L2 MAC-VRF** service (`mac-vrf-evpn-vxlan`) and the ESI/trunk access + VLAN→VNI snips are **Junos-exclusive** on the leaves. The **L3 VRF** service (`vrf-evpn-ip-prefix`) exists on BOTH OS families (Junos leaves + Junos/EVO border-leaves). Spines carry only the shared underlay + EVPN overlay.

---

## Fabric / underlay defaults (eBGP Clos)

| Variable | Default | Notes |
|----------|---------|-------|
| `$ROUTER_ID` | = device loopback | per device (see table), `/32` |
| `$LO_IPV4` | `192.168.255.<n>/32` | lo0.0 primary (n per table) |
| `$LO_IPV6` | `fdf6:ed70:1fac:f2d1::<n>/128` | lo0.0 v6 |
| `$AS_NUMBER` | spine `64512`; each leaf unique `64515`+ | eBGP Clos — every device has its own AS |
| `$PEER_AS` | `64512` | underlay + overlay peer AS toward the spines |
| `$PEER_IP` / `$LOCAL_ADDR` | `10.0.0.<x>` /31 | fabric uplink p2p addressing |
| `$PEER_LOOPBACK` | `192.168.255.0` | EVPN overlay peer (spine loopback) |
| `$LOCAL_LOOPBACK` | = device loopback | EVPN overlay local-address |
| `$VTEP_SHARED_TUNNEL` | `default` | shared-tunnels VTEP source-interface |
| `$SPEED` | `100g` (fabric) / `10g` (access) | port speed |

---

## Interface / access defaults

| Variable | Default | Notes |
|----------|---------|-------|
| `$FABRIC_INTF` | `et-0/0/0` | first spine-facing uplink |
| `$LAG_NAME` | `ae1` | ESI access LAG to multihomed servers |
| `$LAG_MEMBER` | `xe-0/0/1` | first LAG member port |
| `$ESI_VALUE` | `00:01:01:01:01:01:01:01:01:01` | MUST match on both ESI leaves of a multihomed pair |
| `$LACP_SYSTEM_ID` | `01:01:01:01:01:01` | MUST match on both ESI leaves |
| `$ANYCAST_MAC` | `00:1c:73:00:00:01` | shared IRB anycast MAC (Junos) |
| `$AGGREGATED_COUNT` | `2` | chassis aggregated-devices count |
| `$FLEX_INTF` | `et-0/0/8:0` | border-leaf external breakout |
| `$VLAN_RANGE` | `299-399` | external-vlan-tagged range |

---

## Service — L2 MAC-VRF (`mac-vrf-evpn-vxlan`) defaults

| Variable | Default | Notes |
|----------|---------|-------|
| `$INSTANCE_NAME` | `evpn-1` | vlan-aware MAC-VRF instance |
| `$RD` | `<loopback>:65534` | route-distinguisher (per device) |
| `$VNI_TARGET` | `target:10400:1` | per-VNI route-target |
| `$GLOBAL_VRF_TARGET` | `target:100:100` | instance-level vrf-target |
